# Maina — Full Documentation for LLMs

## Overview

Maina is a verification-first developer operating system. It is a CLI tool, MCP server, and skills package that proves AI-generated code is correct before it merges.

AI writes 41% of code today. That code ships with 1.7x more defects than human-written code. Maina closes the verification gap with three engines that observe your codebase, learn your conventions, and verify every change.

## Architecture — Three Engines

### Context Engine (Observes)
4-layer retrieval system:
- Working layer: current branch, touched files, verification status
- Episodic layer: compressed PR summaries with Ebbinghaus decay
- Semantic layer: tree-sitter AST entities + PageRank dependency graph
- Retrieval layer: Zoekt/ripgrep code search

Dynamic token budget: 40% focused (commit), 60% default, 80% explore (context). Each command declares what context it needs.

### Prompt Engine (Learns)
- Constitution (.maina/constitution.md): non-negotiable project rules injected into every AI call
- Custom prompts (.maina/prompts/): per-task markdown templates
- Prompt versioning: every prompt hashed, usage tracked
- A/B testing: candidates auto-promoted at >5% improvement
- Feedback loop: accept/reject drives evolution via maina learn

### Verify Engine (Verifies)
16-tool parallel pipeline:
1. Syntax guard (Biome/ruff/go vet/clippy/dotnet format/Checkstyle) — language-aware, <500ms
2. Semgrep — 2000+ SAST rules
3. Trivy — dependency CVE scanning
4. Secretlint — secret detection
5. SonarQube — quality gates
6. Stryker — mutation testing
7. diff-cover — coverage on changed lines
8. AI review — semantic code review (cross-function consistency, edge cases)
9. Slop detector — empty bodies, hallucinated imports, console.log, TODOs
10. Visual regression — Playwright screenshot comparison

All tools auto-detected and auto-skipped if not installed. Diff-only filtering: only findings on changed lines.

## Multi-Language Support

Automatic language detection from project marker files:
- TypeScript: tsconfig.json → Biome
- Python: pyproject.toml/requirements.txt → ruff
- Go: go.mod → go vet
- Rust: Cargo.toml → clippy
- C#: .sln/.csproj → dotnet format
- Java/Kotlin: pom.xml/build.gradle → Checkstyle

Each language has specific slop patterns (console.log for TS, print() for Python, fmt.Println for Go, etc.)

## Workflow

brainstorm → ticket → plan → design → spec → implement → verify → review → fix → commit → review → pr → merge → learn

Each step records async RL feedback. Workflow context forwarded between steps via .maina/workflow/current.md.

## AI Delegation Protocol

When running inside Claude Code, Codex, or OpenCode without an API key, maina outputs structured request blocks:

```
---MAINA_AI_REQUEST---
task: ai-review
context: Reviewing diff for semantic issues
expected_format: json
prompt: |
  Review this diff...
---END_MAINA_AI_REQUEST---
```

Any MCP-compatible host agent can parse and process these.

## Benchmark

Validator library benchmark (95 hidden edge-case tests):
- Maina: 95/95 (100%)
- SpecKit: 95/95 (100%)

## Installation

```bash
bun add -g @mainahq/cli
maina init --install
maina doctor
```

Requires: Git + Bun. Optional: Semgrep, Trivy, Secretlint, SonarQube, Playwright.

## Package Names

- @mainahq/cli — CLI (the main package users install)
- @mainahq/core — Core engines (Context, Prompt, Verify)
- @mainahq/mcp — MCP server for IDE integration
- @mainahq/skills — Cross-platform skills for AI coding tools
